title: The Pilot's Tally
passage:
For three centuries the pilots who worked the Verran coast steered by a craft the chartmakers rather looked down on. They kept no fixed longitude and trusted no single instrument. Instead each pilot carried a worn logbook — a tally — in which, watch by watch, he set down the ship's heading, his own guess at her speed, and the hours she had run, and from these figures he reckoned by arithmetic alone how far the vessel had crept along an unseen line, east or west, since her last sure landmark. The method had a name the guild used without embarrassment: dead reckoning. It was, on the face of it, a chain of guesses. A misjudged current, an over-generous estimate of speed, a helmsman's drift while the pilot slept — any one of these could throw the whole reckoning off by a day's sail, and no chart aboard could catch the error.

The standard account of how this navigation grew safer is a story about instruments. On that telling, the coast was tamed as better tools arrived — the log-line to measure speed, the traverse board to record each change of heading, and at last the sea-going clock — each one replacing some part of the pilot's fallible senses with a reading he could not fudge. There is real evidence for the story. The charts engraved after 1680, when a workshop at Halden began printing plates instead of selling hand-copied sheets, are demonstrably more consistent with one another than anything the coast had known before, and wrecks on the worst headlands did grow rarer across the same decades.

However, when the historian Ottilie Brandt worked through the surviving tallies of the Verran guild — some six hundred of them, kept together in a single harbour archive — she found the instruments arriving later than the story needs them to. The reckoning grew dependable, she argues, a full generation before the good clocks did. What changed first was not the pilots' tools but their habit of pooling what each of them knew. From about 1650 the guild required every returning pilot to copy his run into a common book, so that a young man setting out could read how forty predecessors had judged the very same stretch of water in the very same season. Where one tally erred, the forty tended to average the error away.

Brandt is careful not to claim too much. "The shared book did not make any single voyage more accurate," she writes. "It made the guild's collective estimate less wrong, which is a slower and humbler thing." The distinction matters, because a pilot out at sea still had only his own reckoning to steer by on the night it counted. And the pooled record carried a bias of its own: voyages that ended in wreck seldom came back to be copied into the book, so the common record quietly favoured the routes and the seasons that had merely happened to be survivable. The east–west problem — fixing how far along the line a ship had truly run — was never really solved by the tallies. It was, at best, hedged: narrowed from a wild guess to an educated one.

So the tidy lesson — that finer instruments made the coast safe — survives only in part. The sea-clock, when it finally came, did what no logbook could, and Brandt does not pretend otherwise. But the record of the Verran pilots suggests that the first real gains came from a plainer source: not a sharper measurement of any single run, but the patient stacking of many ordinary ones, each of them mediocre, until the average at last held steady. What later looked like a triumph of the instrument was, for one crucial century, a triumph of the archive. The pilots learned the line not by measuring it better, but by remembering it together.
— Marta Løvold

dead reckoning = estimating a ship's position from her recorded heading, speed, and time elapsed, without landmarks or outside fixes

questions.prompt: What is this text mainly about?

options.text: That better navigational instruments — the log-line, the traverse board, and eventually the sea-clock — were what gradually made the Verran coast safe to sail.
That the Verran pilots' first gains in reliable reckoning owed less to better instruments than to pooling many ordinary logs until their average steadied.
That the engraved chart plates printed at the Halden workshop after 1680 proved far more consistent than the hand-copied sheets they replaced.
That dead reckoning was such an unreliable chain of guesses that no pilot could trust it at all until the sea-clock at last replaced it.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-004 (main idea / whole-text gist), edge position 1. STEM-LAW: 'What is this text mainly about?' names no content and does not entail the answer. WHY-KEY (B): the arc runs episode (the pilots' dead-reckoning craft) -> context (the standard instruments-made-it-safe account, with 'real evidence for the story') -> reinterpretation (Brandt: 'The reckoning grew dependable ... a full generation before the good clocks did. What changed first was not the pilots' tools but their habit of pooling what each of them knew') -> verdict, paragraph 5: 'the first real gains came from a plainer source: not a sharper measurement of any single run, but the patient stacking of many ordinary ones ... until the average at last held steady', 'a triumph of the archive'. B paraphrases that hedged, two-sided verdict ('owed less to ... than to') without lifting a distinctive four-word run. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = scope_error / half-true reversal — this is the standard account the text explicitly qualifies ('survives only in part'), promoting the instrument story the essay argues was NOT the first driver; tempting because paragraph 2 grants it 'real evidence'. C = detail-as-main / wrong_location — accurately reports the paragraph-2 sub-point about the Halden plates, but that is one strand of the context, never the whole-text focus; tempting because it is a concrete, dated fact. D = surface_word_match / quantifier_upgrade — recycles the paragraph-1 'chain of guesses' image but absolutises it ('no pilot could trust it at all until ... replaced it'), a claim the text refutes, since the reckoning 'grew dependable' a generation before the clocks; tempting to a reader who stops at paragraph 1's warning.

questions.prompt: According to the text, what did the Verran guild begin requiring of its pilots from about 1650?

options.text: That each returning pilot copy his own run into a common book that later pilots could read before sailing the same waters.
That each pilot set down the ship's heading, his guess at her speed, and the hours run, watch by watch, in his personal tally.
That every pilot submit his reckonings to a royal examiner, who checked each one against the newly engraved charts.
That every pilot give up his own reckoning at sea and steer only by the averaged figures written in the common book.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. STEM-LAW: the stem names that a requirement began around 1650 but not its content. WHY-KEY (A): paragraph 3 states 'From about 1650 the guild required every returning pilot to copy his run into a common book, so that a young man setting out could read how forty predecessors had judged the very same stretch of water'. A paraphrases that pooling requirement. WHY-DISTRACTORS: B = wrong_location / time-shift — this is the individual dead-reckoning method described in paragraph 1 ('he set down the ship's heading, his own guess at her speed, and the hours she had run'), which long predates and is not the 1650 change the stem asks about; tempting because it is a faithful detail from elsewhere in the passage. C = outside_knowledge — a royal examiner and a chart-checking step appear nowhere in the text; tempting because it sounds like a plausible guild procedure. D = quantifier_upgrade / contradiction — inflates the pooling into an absolute mandate ('give up his own reckoning ... steer only by the averaged figures'), which paragraph 4 directly contradicts: 'a pilot out at sea still had only his own reckoning to steer by on the night it counted'; tempting because it takes the averaging idea to its apparent extreme.

questions.prompt: What does the text say was a limitation of the pilots' common book?

options.text: The book was so unreliable that a young pilot could learn nothing useful from the runs his predecessors had entered.
The engraved plates from Halden repeatedly contradicted the figures the pilots had entered into the book by hand.
Voyages that ended in wreck were seldom copied back into it, so the book quietly favoured routes and seasons that happened to be survivable.
Pooling the logs made each individual voyage less accurate than a single careful reckoning would have been on its own.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. STEM-LAW: the stem names that the book had a limitation but not which one. WHY-KEY (C): paragraph 4 states 'the pooled record carried a bias of its own: voyages that ended in wreck seldom came back to be copied into the book, so the common record quietly favoured the routes and the seasons that had merely happened to be survivable'. C paraphrases that survivorship bias. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = quantifier_upgrade — inflates the limitation into total worthlessness ('learn nothing useful'), which contradicts the text's claim that where one tally erred 'the forty tended to average the error away' and the reckoning 'grew dependable'; tempting because it takes a stated flaw to an extreme. B = surface_word_match / outside_knowledge — recycles the Halden plates from paragraph 2 into a conflict the text never reports; tempting because both the plates and the handwritten book are real elements of the passage. D = too_far / reversal — Brandt says the shared book 'did not make any single voyage more accurate', not that it made voyages LESS accurate than a lone reckoning; the distractor flips a stated non-improvement into an active harm; tempting because it echoes the accuracy language of paragraph 4.

questions.prompt: What does Brandt's distinction between a 'more accurate' voyage and a 'less wrong' collective estimate imply?

options.text: That the shared book did not make any single voyage more accurate than it had been before the book existed.
That collective estimates are always more trustworthy than a single navigator's individual judgement at sea.
That once the common book existed, individual pilots no longer made reckoning errors of any real consequence.
That the pooling improved what the guild knew as a whole, while a pilot on a given night still had to trust his own reckoning.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-002 (inference / implication), one logical inch beyond the text. STEM-LAW: the stem quotes the distinction but leaves the implication to be drawn. WHY-KEY (D): the distinction ('It made the guild's collective estimate less wrong ... a slower and humbler thing') is glossed in the next sentence — 'because a pilot out at sea still had only his own reckoning to steer by on the night it counted'. D infers exactly the individual-versus-collective gap the passage sets up, without overstating it. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = too_literal — a near-verbatim restatement of Brandt's quoted clause; it retrieves the words but performs no inference, and the stem asks what the distinction implies; tempting because it is unarguably true to the text. B = outside_knowledge / quantifier_upgrade — a general truism ('always more trustworthy') the passage never asserts and its wreck-survivorship bias in fact undercuts; tempting to a reader who reads the averaging as a blanket endorsement of crowds. C = quantifier_upgrade — inflates the collective gain into the absolute claim that individual error effectively vanished, contradicting 'a pilot ... still had only his own reckoning to steer by'; tempting because it optimistically extends the pooling benefit.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's attitude towards the account that finer instruments made the Verran coast safe?

options.text: Convinced: the writer fully endorses the standard story that better instruments, above all else, were what made the coast safe.
Partly persuaded: the writer grants that the sea-clock did what no logbook could, yet credits the first real gains to the pilots' shared archive.
Dismissive: the writer treats the whole idea that instruments mattered as a myth with no real evidence behind it.
Detached: the writer lays out the instrument account and the archive account side by side without favouring either one.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-005 (stance / tone / attitude), edge position 5; signature trap is attribution/scope swap of the essay's own qualified verdict. STEM-LAW: the stem names the target account but not the writer's stance. WHY-KEY (B): paragraph 5 delivers a two-sided verdict — 'the tidy lesson ... survives only in part. The sea-clock, when it finally came, did what no logbook could, and Brandt does not pretend otherwise. But ... the first real gains came from a plainer source ... a triumph of the archive.' 'Partly persuaded' names exactly this bounded position. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = scope overshoot — assigns the writer the full instrument-triumph view the essay explicitly qualifies ('survives only in part'); tempting because paragraph 2 grants that account 'real evidence' before the pivot. C = polarity overshoot — pushes the writer's reservation into flat dismissal, contradicted by 'There is real evidence for the story' and 'Brandt does not pretend otherwise'; tempting to a reader who reads the archive verdict as a total rejection of instruments. D = tone_misread — 'without favouring either' misses the verdict that the first real gains were 'a triumph of the archive'; tempting because the middle paragraphs do weigh both sides before the essay lands on one.

fictional_entities: the Verran coast and the Verran pilots' guild (invented)
Ottilie Brandt, maritime historian (invented; all findings and quotes invented)
generator_meta.question_geometry: Q1 main idea (edge, pos1) / Q2 detail (1650 requirement) / Q3 detail (book's survivorship-bias limitation) / Q4 inference (individual vs collective) / Q5 stance (edge, pos5)
generator_meta.key_letters: B, A, C, D, B — all four letters used, no monotonic run, no fixed alternation; keys mix confident-specific (Q2, Q3) with qualified (Q1, Q5) and balanced (Q4) so 'pick the hedged answer' does not score blind
generator_meta.engineered_traps: quantifier_upgrade planted at Q2/D ('averaged the error away' -> 'steer ONLY by the averaged figures'), Q3/A ('a bias of its own' -> 'learn NOTHING useful'), Q4/C (collective gain -> 'no errors of any consequence'); wrong_location/time-shift at Q2/B (para-1 personal method offered as the 1650 rule) and Q3/B (Halden plates); too_literal at Q4/A (verbatim restatement of the quote as a non-inference trap); scope/polarity swaps of the essay's qualified verdict at Q5/A and Q5/C
generator_meta.law11_note: No 'agreement/bäst' item in this unit; the two detail items (Q2, Q3) use wrong_location distractors that are true elsewhere but identifiably wrong to the stem's scope (1650 requirement / stated limitation), not verbatim-true-to-the-stem details.
generator_meta.schema_note: Emits 5 questions per the long_passage_5q blueprint (ELF long passages are invariantly 5q); this intentionally exceeds candidate-item.schema.json questions.maxItems=4, which was authored for single-question seeded eval items. All other schema constraints pass as-is.
